44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.

165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```

206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.

134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 8	/-!
9	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
10	
11	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
12	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
13	base change on the target.
14	
15	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
16	```
17	  X' --g'--> X
18	  |f'        |f
19	  v          v
20	  S' --g---> S
21	```
22	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
23	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
24	
25	The three main declarations are:
26	
27	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
28	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
29	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
30	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
31	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
32	  associativity).
33	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
34	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
35	
36	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
37	
38	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
39	Tag 02KH.
40	-/
41	
42	universe u
43	
44	open CategoryTheory Limits
45	
46	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
47	
48	open Scheme.Modules
49	
50	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
51	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
52	
53	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
54	
55	Given a commutative square
56	```
57	  X' --g'--> X
58	  |f'        |f
59	  v          v
60	  S' --g---> S
61	```
62	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
63	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
64	
65	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
66	```
67	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
68	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
69	```
70	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
71	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
72	commutativity of the square.
73	
74	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
75	base-change diagram. -/
76	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
77	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
78	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
79	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
80	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
81	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
82	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
83	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)

353	/-- **Counit of the tilde–Γ adjunction is a basic-open isomorphism whenever the restriction
354	of the global sections is a localization.** For a sheaf of modules `N` on `Spec R` and `a : R`,
355	if the structure-sheaf restriction map `Γ(N, ⊤) → Γ(N, D(a))` (read in `ModuleCat R` via the

359	the *only* nontrivial input (`Γ(N, D(a)) = Γ(N, ⊤)[1/a]`) and discharges the rest — the comparison

361	localization `toOpen` and the restriction), hence an isomorphism by the uniqueness of localized

363	the level of `fromTildeΓ`. -/
364	lemma fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule {R : CommRingCat.{u}} (N : (Spec R).Modules)
365	    (a : R)

367	      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.map
368	        (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom] :
369	    IsIso (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app N.fromTildeΓ (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)) := by
370	  -- The underlying function of `Hom.app` agrees (by `rfl`) with that of the `modulesSpecToSheaf`
371	  -- image, where the tilde–Γ triangle identity `toOpen_fromTildeΓ_app` applies.
372	  rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
373	  have hfun : ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom
374	        (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app N.fromTildeΓ (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a))) =
375	      ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom
376	        ((modulesSpecToSheaf.map N.fromTildeΓ).1.app (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)))) :=
377	    rfl
378	  rw [hfun]
379	  -- Abbreviations: `j` is the tilde localization map, `ρ` the structure-sheaf restriction, both
380	  -- localizations of `Γ(N, ⊤)` at `powers a`; `L` is the section map of the counit.
381	  set j := (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤))
382	    (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)).hom with hj
383	  set ρ := ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.map
384	    (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom with hρ
385	  haveI hρinst : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers a) ρ := by rw [hρ]; infer_instance
386	  clear_value ρ
387	  set L := ((modulesSpecToSheaf.map N.fromTildeΓ).1.app
388	    (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a))).hom with hL
389	  -- The triangle identity gives `L ∘ₗ j = ρ`.
390	  have htri : L ∘ₗ j = ρ := by
391	    have := Scheme.Modules.toOpen_fromTildeΓ_app N (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)
392	    apply_fun ModuleCat.Hom.hom at this
393	    simpa [hL, hj, hρ, ModuleCat.hom_comp] using this
394	  -- `L` equals the canonical iso between the two localizations of `Γ(N, ⊤)`, hence bijective.
395	  set ej := IsLocalizedModule.iso (Submonoid.powers a) j with hej
396	  set eρ := IsLocalizedModule.iso (Submonoid.powers a) ρ with heρ
397	  let e := ej.symm.trans eρ
398	  have hcomp : e.toLinearMap ∘ₗ j = ρ := by
399	    ext x
400	    simp only [e, hej, heρ, LinearMap.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_coe,

403	  have hLeq : L = e.toLinearMap :=
404	    IsLocalizedModule.ext (Submonoid.powers a) j (IsLocalizedModule.map_units ρ)

406	  rw [show ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom ((modulesSpecToSheaf.map N.fromTildeΓ).1.app
407	    (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)))) = ⇑L from rfl, hLeq]
408	  exact e.bijective

532	hypothesis `hloc` (each structure-sheaf restriction `Γ(N,⊤) → Γ(N,D(a))` is a localization at
533	`powers a`) is discharged here by the `D(a)`-level transport. See blueprint
534	`lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`. -/
535	noncomputable def pushforward_spec_tilde_iso {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}}
536	    (φ : R ⟶ R') (M : ModuleCat.{u} R') :
537	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M) ≅
538	      tilde ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M) := by
539	  apply pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule φ M
540	  intro a
541	  -- STRATEGY (`of_linearEquiv`): the restriction map `ρ : Γ(N,⊤) → Γ(N,D(a))` of
542	  -- `N := (Spec φ)_* (M^~)` is, under the isomorphisms
543	  -- `gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) ⊤` and `gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) (D a)`,
544	  -- identified with the restriction of scalars along `φ` of the `R'`-side tilde restriction
545	  -- `σ : Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(φ a))`. That `σ` is a localization at `powers (φ a)`
546	  -- (`tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule`), so its restriction of scalars along `φ` is a
547	  -- localization at `powers a` (`IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars`); transporting along
548	  -- the two isomorphisms then yields `hloc(a)`.
549	  -- The two bricks for this discharge are now in place and axiom-clean:
550	  --   • `gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) U : Γ(N, U) ≅ restrictScalars φ (Γ(M^~, (Spec φ)⁻¹ U))`
551	  --     — the open-indexed `e_{D(a)}` isomorphism (blueprint movement (1)); and
552	  --   • `tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule M (φ a)` — the `R'`-side restriction
553	  --     `Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(φ a))` is a localization at `powers (φ a)`.
554	  -- The `of_linearEquiv` finish is: with `e₁ = gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) ⊤` and
555	  -- `e₂ = gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) (D a)`, the naturality square
556	  -- `e₂.hom ∘ ρ = (restrictScalars φ σ) ∘ e₁.hom` (where `σ` is the `R'`-side restriction and
557	  -- `ρ` the pushforward-side one) gives `ρ = e₂.symm ∘ₗ (restrictScalars φ σ) ∘ₗ e₁`; then
558	  -- `IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars` upgrades `σ` (localization at `powers (φ a)`,
559	  -- since `algebraMapSubmonoid R' (powers a) = powers (φ a)` along `φ.hom.toAlgebra`) to a
560	  -- localization at `powers a` of `restrictScalars φ σ`, and two applications of
561	  -- `IsLocalizedModule.of_linearEquiv` / `of_linearEquiv_right` transport this to `ρ`.
562	  --
563	  -- iter-240 PIVOT (`algebraize`): preimage opens are *definitionally* the basic opens
564	  -- (`AlgebraicGeometry.SpecMap_preimage_basicOpen` is `rfl`), so the `R'`-side restriction
565	  -- `σ` is literally `tilde M`'s restriction `⊤ → D(φ a)`, and `algebraize [φ.hom]` supplies the
566	  -- honest `Algebra ↑R ↑R'`/`IsScalarTower` instances that `powers_restrictScalars` needs.
567	  algebraize [φ.hom]
568	  -- `σ` : the `R'`-side restriction map `Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(φ a))` of `tilde M`.
569	  set σmor := (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.map
570	      (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen (φ.hom a) ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op with hσmor
571	  -- Re-expose the `compHom`-via-`φ` `R`-module/scalar-tower instances (the ones the

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
96	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
97	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
98	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
99	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
100	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
101	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
102	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
103	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
104	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
105	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
106	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{lemma}\leanok
110	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
111	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
113	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
114	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
115	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
116	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
117	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
118	  isomorphism.
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	\begin{proof}
122	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
123	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
124	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
125	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
126	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
127	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
128	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
129	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
130	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
131	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
132	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
133	\end{proof}
134	
135	\begin{lemma}\leanok
136	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
137	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
139	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
140	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
141	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
142	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
147	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
148	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
149	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
150	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
151	  affine opens.
152	\end{proof}
153	
154	\subsection{The affine computation}
155	
156	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
157	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
158	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
159	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
160	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
161	
162	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
163	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
164	their proof sketches.
165	
166	\begin{lemma}
167	
168	\leanok
169	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
170	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
172	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
173	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
174	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
175	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
176	  \[
177	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
178	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
179	  \]
180	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
181	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
182	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{proof}
186	
187	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
188	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
189	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
190	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
191	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
192	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
193	\end{proof}
194	
195	\begin{lemma}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
199	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
201	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
202	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
203	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
204	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
205	  \[
206	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
207	    \;\cong\;
208	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
209	  \]
210	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
211	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
212	  of \(N\).
213	\end{lemma}
214	
215	\begin{proof}
216	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
217	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
218	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
219	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
220	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
221	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
222	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
223	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
224	  equation of
225	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
226	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
227	\end{proof}
228

429	\begin{lemma}
430	% NOTE: The three route-iii supports now have their own pinned blocks above —
431	% `lem:powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core), `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`
432	% (section-level counit engine), and `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional` (the assembly
433	% that takes the per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis). The
434	% unconditional decl named in the `\lean{}` pin below is the one remaining open obligation: it must
435	% supply `hloc` for `N := (Spec φ)_* M^~` and feed it to the conditional form. The sole difficulty
436	% in `hloc` is the structure-sheaf smul carrier wall over `D(a)`, whose element-free resolution is
437	% the `D(a)`-specialization of `lem:gammaPushforwardIso` spelled out in the proof below. Pin stays
438	% on the unconditional decl until the prover closes it.
439	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
440	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
442	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
443	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
444	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
445	  \[
446	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
447	    \;\cong\;
448	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
449	  \]
450	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
451	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
452	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
453	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
454	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
455	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
456	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
457	
458	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
459	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
460	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
461	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
462	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
463	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
464	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
465	\end{lemma}
466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
469	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
470	  lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
471	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
472	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
473	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
474	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
475	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
476	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
477	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
478	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
479	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
480	
481	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
482	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
483	  \[
484	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
485	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
486	  \]
487	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
488	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
489	
490	  \medskip
491	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
492	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
493	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
494	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
495	
496	  \medskip
497	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
498	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
499	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
500	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
501	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
502	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
503	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
504	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
505	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
506	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
507	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
508	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
509	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
510	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
511	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
512	  an isomorphism.
513	
514	  \medskip
515	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
516	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
517	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
518	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
519	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
520	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
521	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
522	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
523	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
524	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
525	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
526	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
527	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
528	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
529	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
530	
531	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
532	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
533	  isomorphism
534	  \[
535	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
536	      \;\cong\;
537	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
538	  \]
539	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
540	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
541	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
542	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
543	  the \(\top\)-level one of
544	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
545	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
546	  \(\top\).
547	
548	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The ring equation grounding this
549	  canonical isomorphism is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
550	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
551	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
552	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
553	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
554	  \[
555	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
556	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
557	    \;=\;
558	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
559	  \]
560	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
561	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
562	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
563	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
564	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
565	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}\).
566	
567	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target
568	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) = M[1/\varphi a]\) is canonically the
569	  \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\)
570	  over \(R'\). Apply the ring-change converse
571	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
572	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
573	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) to
574	  obtain the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\) of the same module,
575	  viewed by restriction of scalars. Transporting that localization property back
576	  across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
577	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields
578	  the localization property at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) on
579	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
580	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
581	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
582	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
584	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
585	  global ring map.
586	
587	  \medskip
588	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
589	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
590	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
591	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
592	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the substance of
593	  movement~(1): the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of
594	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the structure-sheaf
595	  restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this open-naturality that drives
596	  the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the per-\(a\) localization fact
597	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level localization
598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with the naturality
599	  square for the inclusion \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a
600	  section-level naturality square re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\).
601	
602	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
603	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
604	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
605	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
606	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
607	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
608	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
609	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
610	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
611	
612	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
613	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
614	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
615	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
616	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
617	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
618	  % bump.
619	  \medskip
620	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
621	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
622	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
623	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
624	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
625	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
626	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
627	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
628	  needed for the affine lane.
629	\end{proof}

631	\begin{remark}
632	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
633	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
634	  \begin{enumerate}
635	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
636	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
